All right, welcome back. In this lesson, we are going to go deep in the challenges and concerns that may come up when presenting your offer and inviting people to enroll, as well as a concept that we call co-creative closes. So this falls into the territory that most people would deem or call objections. And there's a reason we are going to limit our usage of that word, right? It just kind of creates more of this combative energy that is counter to the safety and empowerment we are looking to establish and maintain to get that yes. Cool. So our goal isn't to overcome objections per se. It's to coach decisiveness by mitigating legitimate concerns while untangling the unconscious resistances that may be popping up along the way. Because learning how to identify these and untangle them becomes an incredibly valuable skill that actually puts us back in service of our prospect versus countering their objections, right, and getting in that combative state that really doesn't serve anything except for possibly forced compliance. So how does resistance show up? People usually fall into the category of deferring, delaying, or distorting. So let's break down all three of these further. So deferring is when they defer their decision-making power to a non-available third party, whether that's a business partner, a spouse, a friend, whoever it is, that person isn't available and that person needs to be consulted for the decision. So this is not to be confused with legitimate urgent of this, right, where they may actually be including partners in their decision, right? This could be totally legitimate and welcomed. So it's not meant to be confused with that or simply wanting to run something by a trusted third party that could even be their coach or an advisor. But this version of it has a very clear appearance and presentation as a shutdown attempt where they're putting a wall between themselves and the opportunity and you're going to have to discern this, right, through your presence, through the tonality they're using. This is what's going to make that clear, whether it is legitimate or being used as an out. So if you want to be 100% sure, you can ask on the application or at the beginning of the call if anyone else needs to be present who may be involved in the decision, right? If you ask this and they said, at that point, I'm empowered to make this decision and then later on on the call, they're trying to defer to another person while you also sense a degree of pressure, anxiety, overwhelm, seeping in, you can be fairly certain that this is being used as deferment. So if you're confident that it's not a delay, that it is totally legitimate and reasonable, you can roll with that flow and make their process completely congruent with that of an empowered buyer. So this doesn't disqualify them. It actually further qualifies them in your view. So you could respond with, I love that. Actually recommend that most of our clients do involve their partner in the decision as having their support and buy-in heading into the program greatly enhances the experience and favorite success. When do you expect you can have that conversation? If it comes up as resistance, however, it's important to know that this doesn't necessarily mean its resistance towards you. So this is not the time to take it personal, start thinking you did something wrong. This is essentially usually a result of their own internal pressure, right? So it's just how they are responding in relationship to a big decision that needs to be made. So there's nothing to be taken personally here. You don't have to get into your own defensiveness or combative energy. In fact, it's better. You don't. And what you want to do is give them what they're using the deferment for, which is to depressurize by offering the out yourself. So give them what they want, use their momentum instead of trying to counter it by getting them to feel unreasonable in that ask. So what that might look like is you responding with, I understand, and I definitely want you to have that support as it's likely going to be crucial for our success together. I do want to be honest, though, I'm sensing that this invitation feels a bit more stressful and charged than it needs to be. And an even more important part of our success is that you feel amazing and confident, confident stepping in. Are you sure this is something you're ready to explore? Right? You're giving them the out yourself versus them having to construct it through their own deferment strategies and you're giving them a very important opportunity to self-check. Is this just overwhelming creeping in and I can just kind of hold that in perspective now or do I actually feel better not exploring this right now? So this is a very important consent checkpoint for them to give if you sense they are feeling this deep pressure and using deferment strategies to escape it. Wherever you can, I do encourage you to not mount pressure and definitely not add shame by asking a series of confronting questions. So these are questions that a lot of salespeople will ask when presented with someone who wants to check in with a spouse or a partner. So they'll ask what do you need to talk to them about? What do you think they would say? Are they nearby and available to join the call? And even worse, why do you need to ask for permission? Are you not empowered to act independently? So these are all questions that essentially reinstill this combative energy. They don't restore that sense of safety or power that but instead make you an adversary. And this is only going to invite more deferment strategies, more attempts for them to escape the call, more attempts at delaying which we're going to talk about in a moment and ultimately not serve anyone and it just doesn't feel good. So there may be some very rare moments where it might be appropriate. What is it that you feel you want to consult them with? Is there something that we can still talk about on our end? Is there a way I can still help you out there? But make sure if you are going to use this line of questioning at all that you have a clear enough trust layer of your relationship where they can receive this question without feeling like you are backing them up in a corner. And essentially not giving them what they want, which is that breathing room, which is why they're using that deferment to begin with. So these are the typical follow up questions that yes, may add some degree of clarity and dig deeper at the root of the problem. But unfortunately, they're also often weaponized and will create even greater pressure and shutdown, which is not what you want, right? That can only force compliance when someone is shutting down, not lead to that empowered close. So likewise, the next one is delaying and delaying may be both a natural part of their process or an out from the pressure that's welled up around the invitation. So that might look like great. I'd love to think about it and let you know over the next few days. So this is a very typical response that one might hear is awesome. Give me some time to think about it and I'll get back to you. So if it's legitimate, if it's part of their process, you can agree with it. Awesome. I totally get that. Just want to make sure do you have everything you need to make a decision, right? And they'll give that a yes or no. Say perfect. Then is it reasonable for you to get back to me by Tuesday with your decision so my team and I can do what we need to do on our end to best support you if you choose to move forward, right? So get agreement over the amount of time or the specific day in which a decision will be made. And then obviously you're going to be using your follow ups. You're going to be checking in and making sure that they still have everything they need to make that decision. If, however, the delay is to depressurize and you're going to presence this based on how you're hearing them present that, right? If they are in this state of overwhelm, anxiety, stress, pressure, and that's obvious to you, you want to give them an opportunity to depressurize. So very similar. I understand. And it's totally cool if you want to take a day or two to make your decision. I do want to be honest though. I'm sensing that this invitation feels a bit more stressful than it needs to be. And then even more important part of our success is that you feel amazing and confident stepping in. Are you sure this is something you're ready to explore? So once again, giving them a chance to gain perspective, reevaluate how they are relating to the offer and ideally reset and regain a degree of safety and empowerment to move forward and continue exploring the opportunity. This is a bit advanced, but don't be afraid to take the offer off the table. If you truly feel like working with them in your program or as a client would be counter to the culture you're creating or you're concerned, legitimately concerned about their ability to succeed, right? If you are sensing that they are too stressed, too anxious, too wound up, too disoriented, to really enhance the culture or really be fun to work with for you or your team and certainly not empowered to get results. This is where taking it off the table or at least proposing that you take it off the table isn't used as a manipulative tactic, right? To get them begging to put you back on. That's not what we're going for here. It's really to create once again, that self-check moment of if that truly does feel more safe. So when you do take it off the table, give a specific time frame, you know, such as six months, and this will help them mitigate where they feel more fear in moving forward or waiting somewhere around half a year to get that opportunity again. All right, last form of resistance that you will typically run into is distortion. And this is where all of a sudden they go incoherent. You feel this extreme sense of overwhelm, anxiety, and this inability to process, right? So this creates this decision paralysis where they might start asking half questions that aren't real questions or just kind of filling up space. And it's just really kind of everything is an expression of this anxious, overwhelming panicky spin and nothing feels coherent. So many of us have had this experience talking to people who go incoherent. Everything is just kind of distorted and not making sense. You will presence to this quite easily if you know what to look for for distortion. So it might present in them automatically starting to formulate half baked objections as well that don't really make sense, half completed sentences that they abandon midway. These are really the clear signs that someone is falling into a distortion trap. And once again, you're going to create spaciousness and bring them back to safety and power by asking. I'm sensing that this is creating a bit of overwhelm for you. And it's important to me that if we move forward, you do so with confidence and clarity. Do you feel like that's possible for you at this time? Or would you feel more comfortable revisiting this in six months? So don't try to blast through distortion, right? Give them what they need, which is spaciousness to really breathe, to really view things from a widened lens instead of this experience of overwhelm where they're just not seeing things clearly. Right? They just need a moment to probably recollect themselves to feel like they have the safety of an out if they truly want it, but then have a moment to tap into what is it I actually do want in this moment. So whether it's delay, deferment or distortion, which you should be able to recognize right now, your best intervention is always going to be to depressurize. Otherwise, you risk them projecting their own self created pressure on you. Right? If you try to blast through that or just keep going, they're going to feel or perceive that you are creating that pressure for them, even though most of it is self created. All right, big topic addressing concerns or what most people would deem to call objections. I do encourage you to limit your usage of that word because it just totally changes the frame of what these actually are. Because the best way to overcome is to overcome at all. It is simply to include meaning as long as it's factual to do so, make their concern consistent with the profile of an empowered buyer or a successful client. So the step one here would be to preempt valid concerns throughout the call. Right? We've spoken about this before where you notice potential misalignment or red flags. Be the first person to call it out and make recommendations around your offer, around the investment options that take those into account and mitigate the concern. And when you do this throughout the call, with every potential red flag you see, you make it far less likely that there will be much to address at this stage of the call. Next is to include versus overcome. So recognize objections or concerns as normal and expected forms of resistance. And when you try to overcome, you create even greater defensiveness and an ensuing power struggle where the only quote unquote pause about comes there as once again, compliance, not an empowered yes. But when you acknowledge or even agree at the validity of what they're pointing to or concerned about and then unpack it and offer them choice, you once again reestablish and invite trust, power, safety and resourcefulness. So your goal and concerns present try to get to the root of it. So most people can to stay on the placeholder or surface version, which doesn't give you the ability to unpack it and get to the core. Because most concerns, including financial, will ultimately be rooted in confidence, either in you, the person delivering the program, or the path, or the program, or their ability to actually go through it and get the result. So if you are in a conversation and you hear, I'm just not sure I'll be able to do this. You can follow up on what part specifically, right? And that will give you real data, a real situation for you to unpack. And then you could coach them through achievability of the process by getting them to tap into their own abilities, capacities and possibilities. Like what is it about this specific part that you are unsure you're going to be able to do? Why don't you feel you could do that? Right? Would it be possible if you approached it this way, right? If you had the support of me and my team in doing this, would that feel more reasonable and achievable? So that allows you to get to the root of it and coach someone back into possibility and resourcefulness around it. If someone says the investment feels a bit scary right now for where I'm at. Once again, you want to ask, can you tell me more? Like what part is scary? Where is this fear coming from? You know, and then you can go deeper, right? If in this 60-day guarantee window, we were celebrating this result together, well, the investment have felt worth it, right? So viewing the investment through the lens of the result you're striving to achieve and also reminding them of the risk reversal within it. That there is a kill switch, that there is a guarantee and that if you're not enjoying that result together, they have nothing to worry about, right? Then you can also ask, when you really tap into it, is it the investment itself that feels scary or the fear that it won't actually help you achieve the results you're hoping for? So get to the core. Is it the financial investment or is it the fear of it not working? If it's the fear of it not working, that allows you to explore those components of the conversation versus swimming around the financial aspects, which never really was the root cause of the problem to begin with. So the most effective and ethical way to coach around investment hesitancy is to bring it back to coaching achievability by unpacking their concerns around either their ability or the program's ability to fulfill on the outcome. This is really where you want to be playing around for 95% of the quote unquote financial concerns or objections that come up. However, if you do sense a very heavy burden on the financial side and a stressful state that's starting to threaten that overwhelm or shutdown, so where there really is real financial stress, fear and burden, your goal once again is to reestablish that sense of safety. With questions, you could ask with no wrong answer, would you feel more relief or less relief if we took X program off the table right now, right? To give them a chance to reoffer consent around exploring this offer, give them a door to escape if it's just too much, right? If they're slipping into this financial stress that isn't going to serve them giving an empowered decision on this call. So this will offer them a chance to zoom out and tune into both divergent paths to feel which one is lighter, more expansive and more true to where their momentum is pushing them to actualize. Important note here, so you're not their financial advisor or their accountant, it's not up to you to ask how much cash they have on hand. That's a great way to break trust or to start suggesting funding options, talking about what credit they might have access to, who they can borrow from, what stuff they can sell. So this is a common and very shitty practice that tends to be disguised as being helpful and being leadership based. My personal opinion on this is pretty blunt, it's predatory and disempowering as fuck when someone starts walking you through different ways to get access to credit, starts walking you through what they might be able to sell just so you could join their program. To me, this is the furthest thing from leadership. So you're not their financial advisor, it's not up to you to ask how much cash they have in their account. If they volunteer that, then so be it. And even if they do volunteer that, I will usually stop someone and say, that's not really my business, that's between you and your partner and your accountant. I'm here to help kind of make this financial investment work, but I really don't need to know this information. So where finances are a legitimate concern, your strongest and most ethical move is to empower them to co-create a new path with you. So the co-creative close, your financial situation is between you, your partner and your accountant. In short, it's none of my fucking business. The only thing I care about is in making sure that if you do choose to enroll, it feels safe, expansive and something you're ready and confident to get. Results in, does that make sense? And not only should it make sense, this is what should have them regaining a sense of trust that you are looking out for their best interest, right? That you don't need to delve into these areas that aren't your business. That you're respecting their boundaries and their sovereignty and want them to remain feeling empowered. So they'll give their answer and then you'll say, with that being said, if you're a yes to this program, I'm open to hearing what you propose. As an alternative payment plan or way to get started, right? So you're not lobbying different payment plans their way, which they can accept or reject. You're giving them a chance to propose something. You could also say your financial situation is personal and not really my business. Had a fairness to our clients and the same fairness we'd extend to you, we can't break any lower. But is there an alternate payment schedule that makes us feel more relaxed and achievable for you, right? Allow them to get resourceful around what might work for them because someone is going to be far more to invest in something that they've dictated, right? They're going to feel a sense of power in dictating their own terms and more likely to invest in what they put on the table. So giving people options to express their power within certain parameters like the co-creative close, like alternative payment plans doesn't undermine your leadership. Instead, it enhances it. As we said, people feel safer embarking on plans. They've had some part in creating then being forced to comply or abide or accept or reject with yours, right? If they put it on the table, you can't really reject what you just proposed yourself. So this is a reason why we want to co-create it by giving them a chance to get resourceful around what would feel good and aligned. So co-creative closes, these can also include additional bonuses of training or coaching that specifically address a skill gap or other prerequisite for succeeding in the core programs. So for example, a lead generation training, if a main concern they have is not enough traffic, that is something you can propose to them. Would it feel good to you if we decided to add this traffic training? We have this Facebook ads course that we also have in our vault. Would that feel good to you? It could also include extended guarantee terms. So if their situation won't allow them to implement right away, you can propose the idea of extending the guarantee window accordingly and give them a chance to respond to how far out would you feel comfortable expanding out that window to make sure you have every chance to put this to use before making a final decision, right? And they're going to give that answer. So once again, they're investing in the term that they've created with you It could also be downsells with rain check pricing or upgrades. So if you have a do it yourself version of your coaching program that they can get started at with a lower investment You can also offer them a 30 day upgrade window where you can maintain the pricing of the full coaching program for them, right? So this is also an example of a co-creative close bonus points when you've co-created new terms such as any of these here additional pricing additional bonuses different payment plans different guarantee terms You can then create a custom checkout page for them in thrive card or sam card or whatever you're using with those custom elements, right? So the page link might literally be special offer for Rai, that's what it might say on top of it It could include a coupon code that is literally their name so that when they get on this page They feel reminded and linked to the fact that they've participated in the creation that it's really been created uniquely for them with them And they are invested in essentially what is being mirrored back on that checkout page Important note on downsells if you're going to downsell based on what is revealed It is so much more powerful When you could be the first one to take the main offer off the table or at least propose the idea of taking it off the table So much trust is going to be built in you saying This is our main program, right? It's $6,000. It's $7,000. But for where you're at, I personally Don't feel comfortable with you making this sizeable of an investment at this stage of your business I'd actually recommend you begin with our done it Do it yourself course you've shown yourself to be very self-reliant self-determined And I feel that you can get those results You know on your own and if you do Within a month feel like you want to upgrade if you feel you're getting momentum and can really use that coaching support We're happy to maintain that pricing for you if you choose to upgrade later so This is what we have on downsells the next lesson Where we're going to wrap it all up is a note on ethical urgency. So not the hardcore persuasion Your discount is only good for the next hour. We only reward fast action takers None of that crap. We're going to talk about ethical urgency That you can include and we're going to go deeper on the concept of the 100 Close. So take a breather and I'll meet you in that next lesson. Bye for now
